patterns.title: Purchaseorder — cross-domain driver
patterns.description: Purchaseorder is a key driver across 4 domains, appearing in 38 findings from 38 analytical angles.
angles: Average daily net profit trend over the last 12 months
Campaign performance across channels and objectives shows zero engagement (clicks) in the specified time window, suggesting either a data gap, campaign inactivity, or misconfiguration.

Comparison of average order size between top 20 longest-delay vs top 20 shortest-delay suppliers, controlling for delay direction; finding notes small but consistent difference in opposite direction of expectation, with no strong correlation implied.
Correlation between SKU diversity and on-time delivery performance among high-volume suppliers
Correlation between on-time delivery reliability and consistency of lateness among high-volume suppliers
Joint distribution of on-time rate and lateness variability among high-volume suppliers
example_findings: Among suppliers with ≥10 purchase orders in the last 12 months, chronically delayed suppliers (175 suppliers, ≥80% delayed orders) and occasionally delayed suppliers (1,825 suppliers, 20–79% delayed orders) both showed zero refunds—total, logistics-related, and customer-preference—despite differing delay rates (4.24 vs. 2.93 avg delay days) and order volumes (35 vs. 37.3 avg orders per supplier).

Among suppliers with at least 10 orders in the past 12 months, 10 suppliers achieved a 0% on-time delivery rate (e.g., SUP-001742, SUP-000135, SUP-001849), and the worst-performing supplier with any on-time deliveries (SUP-000934) had only 3.7% on-time delivery (1 out of 27 orders). The top performers among the lowest-rated group (e.g., SUP-000934, SUP-001293, SUP-000626) still had on-time rates below 11%.

Among suppliers with ≥10 orders in the last 12 months, all high-value suppliers (top 25th percentile by total order value, e.g., SUP-000394 with $32,165) exhibited low on-time delivery rates (≤34%) and high average lateness (≥4.2 days), with no high-value supplier achieving >35% on-time delivery—indicating that high order volume/value does not correlate with better delivery reliability and may even coincide with systemic underperformance.

angles: Average daily net profit trend over the last 12 months

Marketing spend efficiency (revenue per dollar spent) by campaign channel and objective over the last 12 months, with comparison of high- vs low-performing campaigns
Refund incidence by supplier delay classification, controlling for delay severity and volume
Refund incidence by supplier delay classification, volume, and delay severity
example_findings: Among suppliers with ≥10 purchase orders in the last 12 months, chronically delayed suppliers (175 suppliers, ≥80% delayed orders) and occasionally delayed suppliers (1,825 suppliers, 20–79% delayed orders) both showed zero refunds—total, logistics-related, and customer-preference—despite differing delay rates (4.24 vs. 2.93 avg delay days) and order volumes (35 vs. 37.3 avg orders per supplier).
All campaign-channel-objective combinations show NULL average clicks per campaign, indicating no clicks were recorded for any campaigns running between May 17, 2025, and May 18, 2026.

All 16 campaign-channel-objective combinations show zero revenue per dollar spent (avg, median, min, and max = 0.0), indicating no revenue was attributed to any campaigns in the last 12 months despite active spending (207–265 campaigns per combination).

patterns.title: Campaign — cross-domain driver
patterns.description: Campaign is a key driver across 3 domains, appearing in 16 findings from 16 analytical angles.
angles: A/B testing impact on campaign ROI
Absence of A/B test campaigns and zero ROI across all non-experiment channels despite known historical ROI performance
Average daily net profit trend over the last 12 months
Average touchpoint frequency per order by type over the last 12 months
Campaign objective performance in ROI (clicks per dollar), filtered to objectives with ≥10 campaigns
Campaign performance across channels and objectives shows zero engagement (clicks) in the specified time window, suggesting either a data gap, campaign inactivity, or misconfiguration.
example_findings: Non-experiment campaigns had an average spend of $1,001,307.89 and 44 total clicks, yielding an average ROI of 0.0 — meaning no measurable return (clicks per dollar) was achieved. The query returned only one row, indicating no campaigns were identified as A/B test experiments under the specified time window (start_ts ≥ 1747507200000 and end_ts ≤ 1779129600000), so a comparison with experiment campaigns is not possible.

No campaigns ran during active A/B test experiments in the specified time window (April–May 2025), so ROI comparison between experiment and non-experiment campaigns is impossible. Among non-experiment campaigns, all channels (Email, YouTube, Meta, Affiliate, TikTok, Google) showed an average ROI of 0.0 clicks per dollar across 850–912 campaigns each, indicating no measurable clicks relative to spend.

No campaigns ran during active A/B test experiments in the specified time window (April–May 2025), and all non-experiment campaigns across all channels (Affiliate, Email, Google, Meta, TikTok, YouTube) showed an average ROI of 0.0 clicks per dollar, indicating zero measurable clicks relative to spend—despite historical ROI performance showing positive returns—suggesting a critical data or operational gap in experimentation coverage.
